items.question_text: Complete: Do not apply visual separation or issue _____ or VFR-on-top clearances in Class A airspace.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, VFR clearances (in addition to VFR-on-top) are not issued in Class A airspace. The paragraph identifies three prohibitions: visual separation, VFR, and VFR-on-top.

choices.text: VFR
VFR clearances

items.question_text: Which of the following is prohibited in Class A airspace under 7-1-1?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, visual separation, VFR, and VFR-on-top clearances are all prohibited in Class A airspace. IFR speed adjustments and altitude assignments remain standard IFR control actions that are not prohibited.

choices.text: Issuing an IFR speed adjustment to maintain longitudinal spacing.
Assigning a Mach number to an IFR aircraft at or above FL240.
Applying visual separation between two IFR aircraft.
Issuing a revised IFR route amendment.

items.question_text: A controller working Class A airspace receives a pilot request to alter altitude under VFR conditions to get above a cloud layer. The controller issues a clearance to 'maintain VFR conditions above FL350.' This action violates paragraph 7-1-1.
items.explanation: True. Per 7-1-1, VFR clearances may not be issued in Class A airspace. A clearance to maintain VFR conditions is a type of VFR clearance and is prohibited regardless of the reason. The controller should have kept the aircraft on its IFR clearance or issued an IFR altitude change.

choices.text: False
True

items.question_text: What are the three things that paragraph 7-1-1 prohibits in Class A airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, the three prohibitions are: applying visual separation, issuing VFR clearances, and issuing VFR-on-top clearances. These all share the common thread of being visual or non-IFR operations that are inconsistent with the IFR-only nature of Class A airspace.

choices.text: Visual separation, VFR, and VFR-on-top.
IFR flight plans, assigned headings, and speed adjustments.
VFR-on-top, IFR separation, and radar vectors.
Contact approaches, visual approaches, and VFR climbs.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2, a controller may clear an aircraft to maintain VFR conditions when the pilot on an IFR flight plan requests a VFR _____.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2a1, the first condition is that the pilot of an aircraft on an IFR flight plan requests a VFR climb or descent. The pilot's request is a prerequisite; the controller cannot initiate this clearance unilaterally.

choices.text: climb or descent
climb/descent
climb or a descent

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2, which facility type has the noise-abatement condition for VFR-conditions clearances?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2a2, the noise-abatement condition carries the TERMINAL scope marker, meaning only terminal facilities may use it. The pilot-requested VFR climb/descent condition (7-1-2a1) has no scope marker and applies to all facilities.

choices.text: En route facilities only.
Flight service stations only.
Terminal facilities only.
All ATC facilities.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2a2, what condition must exist for a terminal facility to issue a VFR-conditions clearance for noise-abatement benefits?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2a2, the clearance must result in noise-abatement benefits where part of the IFR departure route does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude. It is not enough that noise abatement is merely desirable; the IFR route must not conform to an approved noise-abatement route.

choices.text: Any time the pilot prefers to avoid a populated area, regardless of the departure route.
When the tower requests noise-abatement procedures for a departure.
When the IFR departure route segment does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude.
When the departure airport has a published noise-abatement procedure in the AFD.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2, what phraseology is used when the controller also needs to specify an altitude block for a VFR climb or descent?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2 phraseology, after CLIMB/DESCEND VFR, the controller adds 'BETWEEN (altitude) AND (altitude)' or 'ABOVE/BELOW (altitude)' if an altitude constraint is required. This is distinct from MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS which can use UNTIL or ABOVE/BELOW.

choices.text: MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS BETWEEN (altitude) AND (altitude).
CLIMB VFR, MAINTAIN (altitude) UNTIL FURTHER ADVISED.
CLIMB/DESCEND VFR, BETWEEN (altitude) AND (altitude) or ABOVE/BELOW (altitude).
REQUEST VFR CLIMB/DESCENT APPROVED AT (altitude).

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2b, what triggers the controller's obligation to issue an alternative clearance?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2b, the trigger is the controller's judgment that there is reason to believe flight in VFR conditions may become impractical. This is a controller-judgment standard, not a pilot report, weather report, or automated alert.

choices.text: The pilot reports losing visual contact with the ground.
A weather report indicating ceilings below the MVA.
An automated conflict alert between the VFR-conditions aircraft and another IFR aircraft.
The controller's judgment that flight in VFR conditions may become impractical.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-1-2b phraseology, when VFR conditions may become impractical, the controller states: IF UNABLE, (alternative procedure), AND _____.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2 published phraseology, the controller uses 'IF UNABLE, (alternative procedure), AND ADVISE.' The 'AND ADVISE' prompt requires the pilot to report inability to comply.

choices.text: ADVISE

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3, what four items must approach control issue to arriving VFR aircraft when procedures have been established?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3a-d, the four items are: (a) wind, runway, and altimeter setting; (b) traffic information on a workload-permitting basis; (c) time or place to contact tower; and (d) the aircraft may be instructed to contact approach upon initial contact with tower. The item 'sequencing instructions' is not among the listed items.

choices.text: Wind/runway/altimeter, traffic information, tower contact instructions, and the option to direct initial tower contacts to approach.
ATIS code, vector to final, approach clearance, and landing clearance.
Wind, runway, altimeter, and a mandatory traffic advisory for all known aircraft.
Approach clearance, squawk code, frequency change, and wake turbulence caution.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-1-3b, traffic information is issued on a _____ basis.
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3b, traffic information is issued on a workload-permitting basis. This means it is not mandatory when the controller's workload does not allow it, unlike wind, runway, and altimeter which must be issued unless the ATIS condition is met.

choices.text: workload permitting
workload-permitting

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3d, when may an aircraft be instructed to contact approach control for landing and traffic information?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3d, an aircraft may be instructed to contact approach control for landing and traffic information upon initial contact with the tower. This allows tower to redirect VFR arrivals to approach when procedures have been established.

choices.text: Only after the aircraft has entered the traffic pattern.
Upon initial contact with the tower.
Only after approach control has already issued the ATIS to the aircraft.
When the aircraft is at least 10 NM from the airport.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3, which paragraphs are referenced for approach control service availability and application?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3 references, the paragraph points to 7-6-1 (Application) and 7-6-2 (Service Availability) for additional approach control service procedures.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, which facility type does the visual holding of VFR aircraft procedure apply to?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4, the procedure carries the TERMINAL scope marker, meaning it applies only to terminal facilities. En route facilities are not covered by this paragraph.

choices.text: All ATC facilities.
En route facilities only.
Terminal facilities only.
Flight service stations only.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, what type of geographical fix is preferred for visual holding unless the pilot exhibits local familiarity?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4 note, depicted VFR checkpoints on Sectional Aeronautical and Terminal Area Charts are preferred for visual holding fixes unless the pilot shows familiarity with the local area. If the pilot is locally familiar, other prominent geographical fixes may be used.

choices.text: Any NAVAID regardless of whether it appears on a sectional chart.
Depicted VFR checkpoints on Sectional Aeronautical and Terminal Area Charts.
Only IFR intersections on published instrument procedures.
Airport reference points at uncontrolled fields.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-1-4 phraseology, the holding instruction for VFR aircraft is: HOLD AT (location) UNTIL (time or other condition), _____ (description) HOLDING AT (fix, altitude if known).
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4 phraseology, after issuing the hold instruction, the controller must provide traffic information using the word 'TRAFFIC' followed by a description of the aircraft holding at or proceeding to the fix.

choices.text: TRAFFIC

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, which of the following must be done in addition to clearing an aircraft to a visual holding fix?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4b, when an aircraft is cleared to hold at a fix, traffic information must be issued to any aircraft cleared to hold at the same fix. This ensures pilots know about other aircraft sharing the holding location.

choices.text: Issue an IFR altitude assignment.
Provide expected approach clearance time.
Issue traffic information to other aircraft cleared to hold at the same fix.
Alert the tower that VFR holding is in progress.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, what FAA Order paragraph covers visual holding points, as referenced in the note?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4 references, the paragraph points to 4-6-5 for Visual Holding Points and 7-6-5 for Holding. These cross-references provide additional procedures for holding operations.

choices.text: Paragraph 5-3-4.
Paragraph 4-6-5.
Paragraph 3-5-1.
Paragraph 6-2-3.

items.question_text: How does 7-1-1 differ from the VFR-conditions clearance allowed under 7-1-2?

choices.text: 7-1-1 is a blanket prohibition against all VFR operations in Class A airspace; 7-1-2 permits VFR-conditions clearances only outside Class A under specific conditions.
7-1-1 and 7-1-2 are identical; both allow VFR-on-top in all airspace with pilot agreement.
7-1-1 permits VFR conditions in Class A; 7-1-2 permits VFR-on-top in all airspace.
Both paragraphs prohibit visual separation, but only 7-1-1 prohibits IFR separation.

items.explanation: 7-1-1 Is an absolute prohibition in Class A airspace: no visual separation, no VFR, no VFR-on-top. 7-1-2 allows VFR-conditions clearances under specific conditions, but these clearances are not available in Class A due to 7-1-1.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft at FL330 in Class A airspace requests a VFR climb to FL350 so the pilot can avoid an area of reported moderate turbulence that another pilot described visually. The pilot states they can maintain their own separation in VMC.
items.para_context: Per 7-1-1, do not apply visual separation or issue VFR or VFR-on-top clearances in Class A airspace.
items.question_text: How should the controller respond to this request?

choices.text: Approve the request if the pilot accepts responsibility for separation.
Deny the VFR climb request and instead issue an IFR altitude change to FL350 or an appropriate alternative IFR clearance.
Issue a VFR-on-top clearance since the aircraft is already on an IFR flight plan and can remain VMC.
Apply visual separation and approve the climb while the pilot maintains visual contact.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-1, VFR clearances and VFR-on-top clearances are prohibited in Class A airspace, and visual separation may not be applied. The controller must handle this request using IFR procedures only, such as an IFR altitude change.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-2a, what are the two specific conditions under which a controller may clear an aircraft to maintain VFR conditions?

choices.text: When the controller determines weather is severe-clear and the aircraft is above 10,000 feet MSL, or when the aircraft holds an IFR reservation.

When the pilot requests a VFR climb or descent, or when the clearance will result in noise-abatement benefits where the IFR departure route does not conform to an approved noise-abatement route or altitude (terminal only).
When the pilot has ATIS and the airport is VFR, or when the aircraft is operating in uncontrolled airspace.
When the aircraft is on a published SID that includes a VFR transition, or when the tower requests a VFR release.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-2a, the two conditions are: (1) pilot of IFR aircraft requests a VFR climb/descent, or (2) terminal noise-abatement benefit where the IFR route does not conform to an approved noise-abatement route or altitude.

items.situation: A terminal controller clears an IFR departure to maintain VFR conditions for noise-abatement reasons, but the IFR departure route published in the facility SOP already conforms to the FAA-approved noise-abatement route. A trainee questions whether the clearance is valid under 7-1-2a2.
items.para_context: Per 7-1-2a2 (TERMINAL), the clearance must result in noise-abatement benefits where part of the IFR departure route does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude.
items.question_text: Is the clearance valid under 7-1-2a2?

choices.text: Yes, because any noise-abatement concern justifies the clearance regardless of conformity.
No, because the condition requires that the IFR departure route does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude. A route that already conforms does not meet this condition.
Yes, because the terminal noise-abatement condition applies at all facilities, not just terminal.
No, because VFR-conditions clearances for noise abatement can only be issued by en route facilities.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-2a2, the noise-abatement condition applies only when the IFR departure route does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude. Since this route already conforms, the condition is not met, and the clearance should not have been issued under this provision.

items.instruction: The controller cleared the aircraft to descend VFR within a defined altitude block. Which pilot readback is correct?

choices.text: Maintain VFR conditions below 5,000.
Descend VFR, maintain 4,000 until further advised.
Descend VFR, between 5,000 and 3,000.
Descend VFR, climbing between 3,000 and 5,000.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-2 phraseology, CLIMB/DESCEND VFR can be followed by 'BETWEEN (altitude) AND (altitude)' to specify an altitude block. MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS with ABOVE/BELOW is a different phraseology variant.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-1-3 references these paragraphs for additional approach control procedures
items.task: Identify the referenced paragraphs covering application and service availability for approach control services.
items.question_text: Which paragraphs does 7-1-3 reference for approach control application and service availability?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3 references, the paragraph points to 7-6-1 for Application and 7-6-2 for Service Availability regarding approach control services.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-3, what does a pilot's use of 'have numbers' actually mean for the controller?

choices.text: The pilot has received the ATIS broadcast and the controller may omit all landing information.
The controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter if the procedures allow it, but it does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast.
The pilot is reporting the current ATIS code to the controller.
The pilot has all known traffic information and the controller must not issue additional advisories.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-3 and its note, 'have numbers' may support omitting wind, runway, and altimeter, but it does not indicate that the pilot received the ATIS broadcast. The ATIS code and 'have numbers' are separate concepts.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft unfamiliar with the area needs to be held near the airport. The controller considers using a prominent lake clearly visible from the air that is not a charted VFR checkpoint.
items.para_context: Per 7-1-4 and note, depicted VFR checkpoints are preferred unless the pilot exhibits familiarity with the local area.
items.question_text: What should the controller use as the visual holding fix for this unfamiliar pilot?

choices.text: Use the lake since it is prominent and recognizable, even though it is not a charted VFR checkpoint.
Select a depicted VFR checkpoint because the pilot does not exhibit local familiarity.
Ask the pilot to pick any geographic feature and hold over it.
Assign the airport as the holding fix.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-4 note, depicted VFR checkpoints are preferred unless the pilot shows local familiarity. Since this pilot is unfamiliar, a depicted VFR checkpoint should be selected.

items.question_text: Under 7-1-4, what two actions must be taken when it becomes necessary to hold VFR aircraft at visual holding fixes?

choices.text: Clear to prominent geographical fixes recognizable from the air, preferably on sectional charts; and issue traffic information to aircraft at the same fix.
Issue an IFR clearance to each VFR aircraft and assign a discrete squawk code.
Coordinate with all adjacent sectors and issue approach clearances.
Assign holding altitudes and speed restrictions to every VFR aircraft in the hold, regardless of traffic at the same fix.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-4a and 7-1-4b, the two required actions are: clear aircraft to hold at prominent, recognizable geographical fixes (preferably on sectional charts), and issue traffic information to aircraft cleared to hold at the same fix. IFR clearances and squawk codes are not required for VFR visual holding.
